metadata.summary: Systematic process for designing a compelling value proposition using the Value Proposition Canvas. Maps customer jobs, pains, and gains against product features, pain relievers, and gain creators to achieve fit and articulate why customers should choose you.

content.title: Value Proposition Design
content.summary: Products fail because they don't clearly articulate why someone should buy. Value Proposition Design is a systematic process for designing a compelling value proposition using the Value Proposition Canvas from Strategyzer. The canvas maps customer jobs, pains, and gains against product features, pain relievers, and gain creators to achieve fit and articulate why customers should choose you over alternatives.

why.p: A value proposition is the promise of value to be delivered. It answers: "Why should I choose you over the alternatives?" Without a systematic process, teams default to feature lists instead of benefits, copy the same messaging as competitors, fail to articulate value to target audiences, or design products with poor fit between offer and customer needs. The Value Proposition Canvas provides the structure to avoid these failures.

list.item: Pre-launch positioning: before writing landing page copy or running ads
After user research: translating raw interview findings into a structured Jobs/Pains/Gains canvas
Competitive repositioning: when a new competitor enters and the current pitch no longer differentiates
Investor pitch prep: the value proposition canvas maps directly to the "Why us?" slide
Feature prioritization: pain relievers and gain creators ranked by severity guide the roadmap
Before any user research — without customer evidence, the canvas is self-flattering fiction
When the product is pivoting mid-development and the customer segment has not been re-validated
As a one-time exercise — value propositions require iteration as the market responds; a canvas frozen at launch is a liability
When the team cannot agree on the primary customer segment — resolve segmentation first

section.name: value-proposition-canvas
section.p: The Value Proposition Canvas from Strategyzer maps two halves: Customer Profile and Value Map. The Customer Profile contains three columns (Jobs, Pains, Gains); the Value Map contains three columns (Products & Services, Pain Relievers, Gain Creators). Fit is achieved when the Value Map directly addresses the Customer Profile.
section.term: What the customer is trying to get done. Jobs fall into three categories: Functional (file taxes, manage projects), Emotional (feel in control, reduce stress), Social (look competent, gain status). For each job, note when it arises, how often, and what outcome is desired.

What blocks or frustrates the customer from accomplishing their jobs. Pain categories include Undesired outcomes (takes too long, costs too much), Obstacles (can't figure it out, no budget), Risks (might fail, could lose data). Score each pain on severity: Extreme (must solve), Moderate (should solve), Minor (nice to solve).

What the customer wants to achieve or would love to have. Gain categories include Required (minimum expectation), Expected (standard for category), Desired (would love to have), Unexpected (delightful surprise). Focus on Expected and Desired gains.
How the product eliminates or reduces a customer pain. For each pain, answer: What specific feature addresses it? How significantly does it help? Relief level: Partial or Complete.
How the product creates a desired customer gain. For each gain, answer: What outcomes does it produce? How does it exceed expectations? Delight level: Expected or Surprising.

rule.statement: Populate the Customer Profile before the Value Map — working in the opposite direction produces self-serving analysis.
rule.rationale: Value-first mapping leads to confirmation bias; customer-first mapping forces the product to respond to real needs.

rule.statement: Rank pains by severity before mapping pain relievers; a product that addresses 5 minor pains but ignores the one extreme pain has poor fit.
rule.rationale: Resource constraint forces prioritization; addressing severity ensures the product solves the highest-impact problem.

rule.statement: Use verbatim customer language in the canvas — "takes forever to sync" is more actionable than "slow synchronization".
rule.rationale: Direct language preserves emotional intensity and avoids abstract paraphrasing that loses signal.

section.name: value-proposition-statement
section.p: A value proposition statement answers "Why should I choose you?" using a formula: For [target customer] Who [has this job/need] [Product name] is a [category] That [key benefit] Unlike [alternative] We [key differentiator].

example.title: Newsletter platform statement
example.text: For independent writers Who want to grow and monetize their newsletter NewsletterKit is an email platform That stays affordable as you scale Unlike Substack or ConvertKit We charge flat fees, not per-subscriber

example.title: Freelance project management statement
example.text: For solo freelancers Who juggle multiple client projects FreelanceOS is project management That includes client portals and invoicing Unlike Asana or Notion Everything you need is in one simple tool

rule.statement: Write 3 value proposition statement variants using the For/Who/Is/That/Unlike/We formula. Each variant should emphasize a different differentiator. Avoid marketing superlatives ("best", "easiest", "most powerful").
rule.rationale: Multiple variants surface different positioning angles; superlatives are legally and emotionally risky.

rule.statement: Test the value proposition statement with 5 target customers before using it in ads; if they cannot identify themselves in "For [target customer]", the segment is too narrow or the language is off.
rule.rationale: Customer feedback validates the segment definition and language clarity before investment.

section.name: fit-assessment
section.p: Fit is achieved across three levels: Problem-Solution Fit (you address real jobs/pains/gains), Product-Market Fit (customers pay and keep using), Business Model Fit (profitable and scalable). Test fit by: Do customers recognize the pains you describe? Do they value the gains you promise? Will they pay for your solution?

rule.statement: Map every gain creator and pain reliever to a specific product feature — if you cannot name the feature, the claim is aspirational, not a real proposition.
rule.rationale: Unmapped claims are undeliverable and create customer disappointment at launch.

rule.statement: Revisit the canvas after 90 days of shipping — pains that seemed extreme at discovery may have been solved by workarounds, revealing new priorities.
rule.rationale: Market response shifts customer priorities; stale canvases drive feature bloat instead of impact.

antipattern.reason: Features describe what the product does; benefits describe what the customer gets. "Cloud storage" is a feature; "never lose your files again" is a benefit.

antipattern.description: Generic value proposition that applies to all competitors.
antipattern.reason: Same messaging as 10 competitors means no differentiation. Specificity requires competitive research and unique positioning.
Without competitive context, "fit" is relative to nothing. Position against alternatives.

antipattern.description: Assuming you know the pains without validation.
antipattern.reason: Self-flattering fiction happens when customer research is skipped. Canvas without interviews produces a list of features, not a value proposition.
Cognitive overload; customers remember at most 3 claims. Prioritize.

antipattern.description: Agents filling the canvas without real user research data will hallucinate plausible-sounding pains that do not reflect the actual market.
antipattern.reason: LLMs cannot distinguish genuine market pain from hypothetical scenarios. Always provide raw interview quotes as input, not summaries.

antipattern.description: VP statement generation tends to be generic when the differentiator is not explicitly named; force the agent to name the specific feature that makes the claim true.
antipattern.reason: Without specificity, the statement could apply to any competitor. Example: "best support" is generic; "support in under 2 hours" is specific.

antipattern.description: Fit assessment by agents is optimistic by default; require the agent to identify the one extreme pain that the product does NOT address well.
antipattern.reason: Agents tend to rate fit as "Strong" across the board. Forcing the agent to name the gap reveals honesty about trade-offs.

antipattern.description: "Gain" and "feature" are conflated by agents — a gain is a customer outcome ("feel in control"), not a product feature ("dashboard").
antipattern.reason: Without the distinction, agents mix product description with customer value. Gain = outcome; feature = mechanism.

section.name: process-steps
section.step: Map Customer Jobs: Functional, Emotional, Social. For each job, note when it arises, frequency, and desired outcome.
Identify Pains: Undesired outcomes, Obstacles, Risks. Score each on severity (Extreme/Moderate/Minor) and frequency (Daily/Weekly/Monthly/Annual).
Discover Gains: Required, Expected, Desired, Unexpected. Focus on Expected and Desired.
Design Pain Relievers: For each pain, name the specific feature that addresses it and relief level (Partial/Complete).
Create Gain Creators: For each gain, name the specific feature that creates it and delight level (Expected/Surprising).
Achieve Fit: Check if the Value Map (products, pain relievers, gain creators) fully addresses the Customer Profile (jobs, pains, gains).
Write Value Proposition Statement: Using the For/Who/Is/That/Unlike/We formula, write 3 variants.
Test with Customers: Share the statement with 5 target customers. If they don't recognize themselves in "For [target customer]", iterate.
